note: These three skills produce judgment-heavy, first-pass analysis rather than structured/checkable data (contrast with terminology-audit's termbase.csv or vocabulary-contract's deterministic findings). Assertions here are deliberately limited to STRUCTURE (required sections present, in order, table-first formatting, correct scope decisions, no invented content beyond what the corpus supports) — not exact content matches. Judge substance qualitatively by reading the output, not by grading against a fixed answer key. Fixture: this repo itself (ia-lessons) — real git history, genuine document-type variety (SKILL.md files sharing name/description frontmatter, plugin READMEs, audit-report templates with recurring sections), and deliberately near-zero tagging/facet system in use, which is itself a useful test: a good run should say so plainly rather than invent a taxonomy the corpus doesn't have.

evals.prompt: Give me a first-pass reconnaissance of the Markdown docs in this repo (ia-lessons) — what's here, how it's organized, and what to look at next.
evals.expected_output: corpus-discovery-report.md with Summary first (file count, fraction of total corpus, first-pass impression), then Content distribution, Repository structure, Observations, Gaps, Out of scope, Recommendations for deeper analysis — in that order. Roughly 400-600 words of prose (tables don't count). Non-.md files (plugin.json, check_terms.py, CSVs) noted as out of scope, not silently ignored or silently included. Recommendations point to markdown-content-model-discovery and markdown-vocabulary-governance for deeper follow-up, not attempt that analysis itself. Should NOT name or group document types (e.g. 'skill files vs report files') — that's content-model-discovery's job, this skill points to evidence of variety instead.
assertions: Report has all 7 required sections in the specified order, with Summary first
Total prose length is roughly in the 400-600 word range (some tolerance is fine; a report several times that length is a finding, not noise)
Non-Markdown files (plugin.json, .py, .csv) are explicitly called out as out of scope, not silently omitted from any accounting or silently treated as in-scope

The report does not name or group content into document types (e.g. does not say things like 'there are skill files and report files') — it points to structural evidence (frontmatter presence, heading variety) instead and defers typing to content-model-discovery
git provenance signals (recent activity, staleness) are mentioned, not just static file counts
Content distribution and Observations are rendered as tables, not prose paragraphs

evals.prompt: What's the implicit content model of this repo's Markdown docs? I want to know what document types exist and what metadata schema they should have.
evals.expected_output: content-model-report.md with Summary, Content types, Recommended schema per type, Missing metadata, Validation rules, Unclassified content — in that order, table-first. SKILL.md files should be recognized as a distinct type (consistent name/description frontmatter). Missing-metadata findings should be calibrated by type — this repo has essentially no facet/tag frontmatter anywhere outside SKILL.md's name/description, so the report should say that plainly for non-SKILL.md types rather than inventing an aspirational schema the corpus doesn't support.
assertions: Report has all 6 required sections in the specified order, with Summary first
SKILL.md files are identified as a distinct, consistent content type (they share the name/description frontmatter pattern)
Validation rules proposed are traceable to something actually observed in the corpus, not aspirational rules with no supporting evidence
The near-absence of tagging/facet frontmatter outside SKILL.md is stated plainly rather than papered over with an invented schema
Content types and schema sections are rendered as tables

evals.prompt: Check this repo's Markdown docs for terminology and tagging consistency — do we call things by the same name, and is there a real taxonomy or is folder placement the only signal?
evals.expected_output: vocabulary-governance-report.md with Summary, Vocabulary Governance, Taxonomy Design, Cross-cutting observations — in that order, exactly these 4 sections and no more. Should surface real candidate terminology tension in this repo (e.g. 'plugin' vs 'skill' used close together, 'termbase' vs 'glossary' vs 'controlled vocabulary'). Taxonomy Design should honestly report that there is close to no frontmatter-based tagging/facet system in this repo — folder placement (plugins/<name>/skills/<name>/) is close to the only organizing signal — rather than inventing facets the corpus doesn't have.
assertions: Report has exactly the 4 required top-level sections, in order, and no extra trailing sections (no appended 'recommendations' block after Cross-cutting observations)
At least one real candidate vocabulary tension specific to this repo is identified (e.g. plugin vs skill, or termbase vs glossary vs controlled vocabulary), not just generic filler
Taxonomy Design accurately reflects that this repo has little to no frontmatter-based facet/tagging system, rather than inventing one
Vocabulary Governance and Taxonomy Design are each usable independently (a reader given only one section wouldn't need the other to understand it)
Canonical-terms and facet tables use the documented column headers, not ad hoc alternatives
